_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against the 2018 AUA Erectile Dysfunction Guideline (Burnett et al. J Urol; amended 2023), 2021 EAU Sexual & Reproductive Health Guideline on Male Sexual Dysfunction (Salonia et al. Eur Urol), Selvin 2007 NHANES Am J Med prevalence data, the Massachusetts Male Aging Study (Feldman 1994), Vlachopoulos 2013 cardiovascular meta-analysis, Goldstein 1998 NEJM sildenafil pivotal trial, Linet 1996 NEJM intracavernosal alprostadil trial, and Carson 2011 inflatable penile prosthesis long-term outcomes. Drug doses and step therapy checked against AUA 2018 (amended 2023) and EAU 2021.

h1Title: Erectile Dysfunction: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Erectile dysfunction is the persistent inability to achieve or maintain a penile erection sufficient for satisfactory sexual performance, driven in most adult men by impaired nitric-oxide-mediated relaxation of the cavernosal smooth muscle and reduced penile arterial inflow. The Massachusetts Male Aging Study (Feldman 1994) and the 2001-2002 NHANES analysis (Selvin 2007) found prevalence rising from about 5% at age 40 to nearly 70% by age 70, with roughly 18 million US men affected. Vasculogenic disease accounts for 60-80% of organic cases and frequently precedes coronary events by 3-5 years (Vlachopoulos 2013). PDE5 inhibitors restore erections in 60-80% of men, with intracavernosal alprostadil, vacuum erection devices, and inflatable penile prostheses available when oral therapy fails.

keyStats.prevalence: ~52% of US men aged 40-70 report some degree of ED (Massachusetts Male Aging Study, Feldman 1994); 18.4% of US men ≥20 (NHANES 2001-2002, Selvin 2007)
keyStats.demographics: Prevalence rises from ~5% at age 40 to ~70% at age 70; doubled in men with type 2 diabetes
keyStats.avgAge: Onset typically after age 40; complete ED is uncommon before 50
keyStats.globalCases: ~150 million men worldwide in 1995; projected to exceed 322 million by 2025 (Ayta 1999 BJU Int estimates)

definition: Erectile dysfunction (ICD-10: N52) is defined by the AUA and the EAU as the consistent or recurrent inability to attain or maintain a penile erection sufficient for satisfactory sexual performance, present for at least three months. The physiology of erection requires intact arterial inflow, venous occlusion within the corpora cavernosa, neural signalling via the pelvic parasympathetic plexus, and a permissive hormonal milieu. Sexual stimulation triggers nitric oxide release from cavernous nerves and endothelium; nitric oxide activates guanylate cyclase to raise cyclic GMP, which relaxes trabecular smooth muscle, expands the sinusoidal spaces, and compresses subtunical venules to trap blood. Phosphodiesterase type 5 degrades cyclic GMP and ends the erection — the target of all oral PDE5 inhibitors. ED is classified by mechanism as vasculogenic (the largest category, comprising arteriogenic and veno-occlusive subtypes), neurogenic (post-prostatectomy, diabetic, spinal cord injury), endocrine (hypogonadism, hyperprolactinemia), drug-induced (antihypertensives, antidepressants, antiandrogens), or psychogenic. Severity is graded with the validated International Index of Erectile Function (IIEF-5 / SHIM), where scores of 22-25 indicate no ED, 17-21 mild, 12-16 mild-to-moderate, 8-11 moderate, and 5-7 severe ED. The condition is managed primarily by urologists, often in collaboration with cardiology, endocrinology, and sexual medicine specialists, as ED frequently signals systemic vascular disease.

typesClassification.description: Reduced penile arterial inflow from atherosclerosis of pudendal and cavernous arteries. The most common organic mechanism in men over 50 and the same disease process that drives coronary artery disease. Penile arteries are 1-2 mm in diameter and become symptomatic before larger coronaries.

Failure of the subtunical venules to compress despite adequate arterial inflow, allowing blood to drain prematurely. Often seen with cavernosal smooth muscle dysfunction, Peyronie's plaque, or after pelvic trauma; diagnosed with duplex ultrasound showing high end-diastolic velocity.

Disrupted neural transmission along the cavernous nerves or spinal pathways. Common after radical prostatectomy or pelvic radiotherapy, in advanced diabetic autonomic neuropathy, multiple sclerosis, and spinal cord injury. Responds poorly to oral PDE5 inhibitors when nerves are completely transected.

Driven by low serum testosterone (total <300 ng/dL on two morning samples), hyperprolactinemia, or thyroid dysfunction. Restoration of testosterone in confirmed hypogonadism can improve erectile response, especially when combined with a PDE5 inhibitor.

Roughly 25% of ED cases are partly drug-related. Common offenders include thiazide diuretics, beta-blockers (atenolol more than nebivolol), SSRIs, finasteride and dutasteride, antipsychotics, and opioids. Swapping the offending drug often improves function within weeks.

Performance anxiety, depression, relationship conflict, or post-traumatic stress, often presenting with sudden situational onset, preserved nocturnal erections, and normal duplex ultrasound. Typically responds to a brief PDE5 inhibitor trial plus psychosexual counselling.

Affects 50-80% of men in the first year after radical prostatectomy, even with nerve-sparing technique. Penile rehabilitation with daily or on-demand PDE5 inhibitors plus vacuum therapy is recommended to preserve cavernosal smooth muscle until neural recovery.

primarySymptoms: Difficulty achieving an erection during sexual activity on more than 50% of attempts over the prior three months — the validated definition used in the IIEF-5 / SHIM questionnaire.
Inability to maintain an erection long enough for satisfactory intercourse, with the penis becoming detumescent before or during penetration.
Erection that is incomplete or insufficiently rigid for penetration despite normal sexual desire and arousal — distinguishing ED from low libido.
Loss of the morning (nocturnal) erections that healthy men typically experience 3-5 times per night during REM sleep — a useful pointer toward organic rather than psychogenic disease.
Reduced or absent response to visual or tactile sexual stimulation, often with shorter duration of any erection that does occur.
Coexisting reduced sexual desire, ejaculatory changes, or premature ejaculation in 30-40% of men with ED — symptoms cluster more often than they occur alone.
Onset that is gradual over months to years in organic vasculogenic ED, versus abrupt situational onset with preserved morning erections in psychogenic ED.
Performance anxiety, avoidance of sexual intimacy, and relationship strain that frequently develop secondary to repeated unsuccessful attempts.
Decreased rigidity even with manual stimulation and during self-stimulation — distinguishing organic ED, which is generally global, from situational psychogenic ED.
Symptoms of low testosterone in endocrine ED — fatigue, loss of body hair, reduced muscle mass, depressed mood, hot flushes — that warrant a morning total testosterone measurement.
earlyWarningSigns: Occasional, intermittent difficulty maintaining an erection during the prior 6-12 months that has slowly become more frequent
Loss of nocturnal or early-morning erections in a man previously aware of regular morning erections
New onset of ED within weeks of starting a thiazide diuretic, beta-blocker, SSRI, or 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor
ED beginning in a man with known cardiovascular risk factors (hypertension, hyperlipidemia, smoking, type 2 diabetes) — the penile vasculature is often the first to fail
Reduced morning erections together with fatigue, low libido, and reduced exercise tolerance — a pattern suggesting hypogonadism
emergencySigns: Painful, persistent erection lasting more than 4 hours (priapism) after intracavernosal injection therapy or PDE5 inhibitor use — a urological emergency requiring corporal aspiration within 6 hours to prevent permanent damage

Sudden chest pain, severe headache, vision change, or breathlessness during or shortly after sexual activity in a man with cardiac risk factors — possible myocardial infarction or stroke; call emergency services

Sudden vision loss, blue-tinted vision, or a sudden hearing loss after a PDE5 inhibitor dose — possible non-arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (NAION) or sudden sensorineural hearing loss; stop the drug and seek care
Severe, unrelieved hypotension or syncope within 24 hours of taking a PDE5 inhibitor together with a nitrate or alpha-blocker — life-threatening interaction requiring emergency department evaluation
Acute, severe penile pain or curvature during intercourse with an audible 'pop' — possible penile fracture, which is a surgical emergency

causes.cause: Endothelial dysfunction and atherosclerosis of penile arteries
causes.description: The single largest cause in men over 50. Cavernosal and pudendal arteries (1-2 mm internal diameter) develop endothelial dysfunction and atheroma earlier than coronary arteries; ED therefore frequently precedes overt coronary events by 3-5 years and confers a 1.4-1.5x increased risk of major adverse cardiovascular events (Vlachopoulos 2013).

Type 2 diabetes triples ED risk through combined neuropathy of cavernous nerves, endothelial dysfunction, advanced glycation end-product accumulation, and reduced nitric-oxide bioavailability. About 50-75% of men with longstanding diabetes develop ED, often a decade earlier than in non-diabetic men.

Radical prostatectomy, radical cystectomy, and pelvic radiotherapy injure the cavernous nerves running on the posterolateral prostate. Post-prostatectomy ED affects 50-80% in the first year even with nerve-sparing technique; recovery continues for up to 24 months.

Thiazides, non-selective beta-blockers, spironolactone, SSRIs, finasteride and dutasteride, antipsychotics, opioids, and antiandrogen therapy collectively account for an estimated 25% of cases. Effects are often dose-dependent and reversible within weeks of switching agents.

Total testosterone below 300 ng/dL on two confirmed morning samples impairs nocturnal erections, libido, and cavernosal smooth muscle responsiveness. Testosterone replacement in confirmed deficiency improves erectile response to PDE5 inhibitors but is not first-line monotherapy for ED.

Current smokers have roughly double the ED risk of non-smokers, mediated by accelerated atherosclerosis, endothelial dysfunction, and impaired nitric oxide synthesis. ED risk falls measurably within one year of cessation.

Performance anxiety, depression, relationship conflict, and post-traumatic stress account for most ED in men under 40. Suggested by sudden situational onset, intact nocturnal erections, and a normal vascular workup. Often coexists with organic causes in older men.

causes.cause: Peyronie's disease and structural penile abnormalities
causes.description: Tunica albuginea scarring causes curvature, plaque palpation, and veno-occlusive dysfunction in 30-50% of Peyronie's patients. Severe ventral or hourglass deformity can mechanically prevent rigid penetration.

riskFactors.description: ED prevalence rises from ~5% at age 40 to ~15% at 70 for complete ED, with any-severity ED reaching ~70% by age 70 (Feldman 1994). Age is the strongest single demographic predictor.

Hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and known coronary artery disease each roughly double ED risk. Men with ED have a 1.4-fold increased risk of major adverse cardiovascular events over 7 years (Vlachopoulos 2013 meta-analysis of 36,744 men).

Triples ED risk; affects 50-75% of men with established diabetes, often a decade earlier than in non-diabetic men. Poor glycemic control and longer disease duration are the strongest within-diabetes predictors.
Current smokers carry roughly a 2x ED risk; effect is dose-dependent and partially reversible with cessation within 12 months.

BMI ≥30 raises ED risk 1.3-1.9 fold via endothelial dysfunction, insulin resistance, and lower testosterone. Mediterranean-style diet and 5-10% weight loss measurably improve IIEF-5 scores in trial data.
Men with less than 30 minutes of moderate activity weekly have approximately 30% higher ED risk than those exceeding 150 minutes per week, after adjustment for BMI and comorbidities.

Radical prostatectomy, radical cystectomy, and pelvic radiation produce ED in 50-80% of patients despite nerve-sparing techniques; risk is higher with non-nerve-sparing surgery, older age, and pre-existing ED.

Thiazide diuretics, non-selective beta-blockers, spironolactone, SSRIs, finasteride and dutasteride, antipsychotics, and opioids contribute to roughly 25% of cases. Swapping the offending drug is often the most cost-effective intervention.

Independently raise ED risk 1.8-2x even after controlling for medication effects. SSRIs add further pharmacologic burden; bupropion or vortioxetine are lower-risk antidepressant alternatives in men with ED.
Untreated OSA roughly doubles ED risk through nocturnal hypoxia, endothelial dysfunction, and reduced testosterone. CPAP therapy improves IIEF-5 scores in many men with confirmed disease.

riskFactors.factor: Spinal cord injury and pelvic trauma
riskFactors.description: Complete suprasacral spinal cord injury preserves reflex but not psychogenic erections; complete sacral injury abolishes both. Bicycle saddle pressure and pelvic fracture are additional traumatic causes.

affectedDemographics: US prevalence: about 18 million men age 20+ in 2001-2002 (NHANES; Selvin 2007); rises sharply with age
Massachusetts Male Aging Study found 52% of men 40-70 reported some degree of ED, 17% mild, 25% moderate, 10% complete (Feldman 1994)
Black and Hispanic men in the US have ED prevalence 2-3 percentage points higher than non-Hispanic White men at equivalent age, partly mediated by higher rates of hypertension and diabetes
Globally about 150 million men were affected in 1995; modelling projected over 322 million by 2025 (Ayta 1999 BJU Int)
Men with type 2 diabetes have 2-3x the prevalence of age-matched non-diabetic peers and develop ED on average 10-15 years earlier
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of erectile dysfunction begins with a focused sexual, medical, and psychosocial history, supplemented by a validated questionnaire such as the IIEF-5 (Sexual Health Inventory for Men, SHIM). The AUA 2018 guideline and EAU 2021 update both recommend the IIEF-5 as the initial standardised assessment; scores below 22 indicate ED, with severity graded by score band. History should establish onset (gradual versus abrupt), presence of nocturnal erections, situational versus global pattern, libido, ejaculatory function, partner factors, and a full medication review. Physical examination focuses on cardiovascular status (blood pressure, peripheral pulses, signs of metabolic syndrome), secondary sex characteristics, genital examination for testicular volume, penile plaques (Peyronie's), and a digital rectal examination in men over 40. First-line laboratory testing is targeted: fasting glucose or HbA1c, lipid panel, and an 8-10 a.m. total testosterone in men with low libido, fatigue, or examination findings suggesting hypogonadism; prolactin, LH, FSH, and free testosterone are reserved for confirmed low total testosterone. Most men with typical ED do not require specialised vascular or neurological testing — empiric trial of a PDE5 inhibitor in correctly counselled patients is both diagnostic and therapeutic. Penile duplex Doppler ultrasound with intracavernosal vasoactive injection is the gold-standard vascular test, reserved for men who fail PDE5 inhibitors, are considering surgical implants, have a history of pelvic trauma, or have legal-medical indications. Nocturnal penile tumescence and rigidity monitoring (RigiScan) distinguishes psychogenic from organic ED but is rarely used outside research and forensic settings. Because ED is an early marker of generalised vascular disease, ACC/AHA-style global cardiovascular risk assessment is recommended in every man at diagnosis, and men with multiple risk factors should be evaluated for occult coronary disease per the Princeton III consensus.

diagnosticTests.test: International Index of Erectile Function-5 (IIEF-5 / SHIM)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Validated 5-item self-administered questionnaire that grades ED severity (none, mild, mild-to-moderate, moderate, severe) and tracks treatment response. Recommended as first-line by both AUA 2018 and EAU 2021 guidelines.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Five questions covering the previous 6 months, completed in under 5 minutes in clinic or at home. Each item scored 1-5; total 22-25 indicates no ED, 5-7 severe ED.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies modifiable contributors: fasting glucose or HbA1c (diabetes), lipid panel (cardiovascular risk), morning total testosterone (hypogonadism), TSH, and prolactin when clinically indicated.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard fasting blood draw, usually obtained at the same visit. Morning testosterone is measured between 8 and 10 a.m. on at least two separate samples before diagnosing hypogonadism.

diagnosticTests.purpose: ED is an early marker of systemic vascular disease. Every new diagnosis triggers blood pressure, lipid, glucose, and pooled-cohort 10-year cardiovascular risk assessment as recommended by the Princeton III consensus.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: In-clinic measurement of blood pressure, calculation of ASCVD risk score, exercise tolerance review, and onward referral to cardiology when intermediate or high risk is identified.

diagnosticTests.test: Penile duplex Doppler ultrasound with intracavernosal injection
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard vascular test. Measures peak systolic velocity (PSV) and end-diastolic velocity (EDV) after a vasoactive injection. PSV <25 cm/s indicates arteriogenic ED; EDV >5 cm/s indicates veno-occlusive dysfunction. Used when PDE5 inhibitors fail or surgery is considered.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Intracavernosal injection of prostaglandin E1 (alprostadil), then ultrasound imaging at 5-minute intervals for 25-30 minutes. Mild discomfort at the injection site; the resulting erection typically settles in 1-2 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Nocturnal penile tumescence and rigidity testing (RigiScan)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes psychogenic from organic ED by recording erections during REM sleep. Healthy men have 3-5 sustained erections per night with axial rigidity above 70%. Rarely used clinically; reserved for medico-legal or research indications.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Wear a small device with two penile rings for 2-3 consecutive nights at home. The device records circumference and rigidity continuously and is returned to the clinic for analysis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: In men with confirmed total testosterone <300 ng/dL on two morning samples: LH, FSH, prolactin, and free testosterone distinguish primary (testicular) from secondary (pituitary or hypothalamic) hypogonadism and screen for hyperprolactinemia or pituitary mass.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single fasting morning blood draw. If prolactin is markedly elevated, pituitary MRI follows.

diagnosticTests.test: Penile colour-doppler ultrasound with dynamic infusion cavernosometry (DICC)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Reserved for selected veno-occlusive ED candidates being considered for surgical correction. Quantifies leak volume and pressure decay; rarely performed today outside referral centres.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Specialist day-case study with saline infusion into the cavernosa under pressure monitoring; performed only after duplex demonstrates suspected venous leak.

treatmentOverview: Modern ED management follows the 2018 AUA Guideline (amended 2023) and the 2021 EAU Sexual and Reproductive Health Guideline, both of which recommend a shared-decision-making model and a stepwise approach. First-line therapy is lifestyle modification combined with an oral PDE5 inhibitor. The four licensed PDE5 inhibitors — sildenafil 25-100 mg, vardenafil 5-20 mg, tadalafil 5-20 mg on-demand or 2.5-5 mg daily, and avanafil 50-200 mg — produce successful intercourse in 60-80% of men across diagnostic categories, with similar peak efficacy but differing half-lives. Tadalafil's 17.5-hour half-life supports daily dosing and the only-licensed continuous treatment of coexisting lower urinary tract symptoms from benign prostatic hyperplasia. Sildenafil, vardenafil, and avanafil have on-demand profiles taken 30-60 minutes before sexual activity. Absolute contraindications include any nitrate use (organic nitrates, amyl nitrate / 'poppers') because of catastrophic hypotension, and caution applies with alpha-blockers, severe hepatic or renal impairment, and recent stroke or acute myocardial infarction. Second-line therapy when oral agents fail or are contraindicated includes intracavernosal injection of alprostadil (10-40 µg) — effective in 70-90% of men in the pivotal Linet 1996 NEJM trial — intraurethral alprostadil pellets (MUSE 125-1000 µg), and vacuum erection devices, which mechanically draw blood into the penis with a constriction ring used for up to 30 minutes. Combination injection therapy (papaverine + phentolamine + alprostadil, 'Trimix') is used in PDE5 non-responders. Testosterone replacement is added in men with biochemically confirmed hypogonadism (total testosterone <300 ng/dL on two morning samples) who have symptoms; isolated low testosterone with normal sexual function is not an indication. Third-line therapy is surgical inflatable penile prosthesis, which produces patient satisfaction rates of 90-95% and partner satisfaction of 80-90% in long-term series and is the most definitive treatment for refractory ED. Penile revascularisation surgery has narrow indications limited to younger men with focal pudendal artery injury after pelvic trauma. Low-intensity extracorporeal shock wave therapy is now classified as 'investigational' by AUA 2018 (amended 2023) — promising trial data exist but the technology is not yet recommended as standard care outside clinical trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Sildenafil (25-100 mg taken 30-60 minutes before sexual activity)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral PDE5 inhibitor and the first agent of the class (Goldstein NEJM 1998). Half-life 4 hours; absorption delayed by fatty meals. Avoid combining with nitrates of any form. Start at 50 mg in most men, 25 mg in older adults or moderate renal impairment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Successful intercourse in 69% versus 22% placebo in the pivotal 1998 NEJM trial; pooled efficacy across indications 60-80%.

medicalTreatments.name: Tadalafil (10-20 mg on-demand or 2.5-5 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Longer-acting PDE5 inhibitor; half-life 17.5 hours allows 'weekend pill' dosing or once-daily continuous therapy. The only PDE5 inhibitor licensed for concurrent benign prostatic hyperplasia symptoms at 5 mg daily.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Successful intercourse in 71-75% on-demand and 67-72% daily across pivotal trials; sustained benefit at 24-36 hour interval.

medicalTreatments.name: Vardenafil (5-20 mg taken 30-60 minutes before sexual activity)
medicalTreatments.description: PDE5 inhibitor with rapid absorption and a half-life of 4-5 hours, available as conventional and orodispersible tablets. Avoid with class IA or III antiarrhythmics due to mild QT prolongation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Successful intercourse in 65-80% in randomised trials including diabetic and post-prostatectomy populations.

medicalTreatments.name: Avanafil (50-200 mg taken 15-30 minutes before sexual activity)
medicalTreatments.description: Newer-generation PDE5 inhibitor with faster onset (as quick as 15 minutes) and a more selective receptor profile, producing fewer visual side effects. Half-life 6-17 hours.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Successful intercourse in 57-83% across phase III trials; comparable peak efficacy to sildenafil with a faster onset.

medicalTreatments.name: Intracavernosal alprostadil injection (5-40 µg, self-administered)
medicalTreatments.description: Second-line therapy for men who fail or cannot tolerate PDE5 inhibitors. Injected into the lateral corpus cavernosum 5-20 minutes before intercourse using a fine 27-30G needle. Trimix (alprostadil + papaverine + phentolamine) is used in alprostadil non-responders.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Successful intercourse in 87% of attempts at the optimised dose in the Linet 1996 NEJM pivotal trial; long-term continuation around 50-60% at 2 years.

medicalTreatments.name: Intraurethral alprostadil pellet (MUSE, 125-1000 µg)
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative second-line option when injections are unacceptable. A small pellet inserted into the distal urethra after urination delivers alprostadil through urethral mucosa to the corpora.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Successful intercourse in 30-60% of attempts; lower than injection therapy but avoids needles.

medicalTreatments.name: Vacuum erection device with constriction ring
medicalTreatments.description: Non-pharmacologic second-line option. A cylinder is placed over the penis and a hand or battery pump creates negative pressure that draws blood in; a constriction ring at the base maintains the erection for up to 30 minutes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Produces an erection sufficient for intercourse in 60-80% of users; satisfaction lower than oral or injection therapy but useful in men preferring drug-free options or in penile rehabilitation.

medicalTreatments.name: Testosterone replacement therapy (gel, injection, or pellet)
medicalTreatments.description: Indicated only in men with confirmed total testosterone <300 ng/dL on two morning samples and symptoms of hypogonadism. Restores libido and improves PDE5 inhibitor response in combined-deficiency states. Contraindicated in untreated prostate cancer and severe lower urinary tract symptoms.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves erectile function modestly when combined with a PDE5 inhibitor in hypogonadal men; no demonstrated benefit in eugonadal men with ED.

surgicalOptions.name: Inflatable penile prosthesis (3-piece IPP)
surgicalOptions.description: Gold-standard surgical treatment for refractory ED. Two cylinders are implanted in the corpora cavernosa, a pump in the scrotum, and a reservoir behind the rectus muscle. The patient inflates the device for intercourse and deflates afterwards. Antibiotic-coated devices reduced infection rates below 2% in long-term cohorts (Carson 2011).
surgicalOptions.successRate: Patient satisfaction 90-95% and partner satisfaction 80-90% in long-term series; mechanical reliability over 80% at 10 years.

surgicalOptions.name: Malleable (semi-rigid) penile prosthesis
surgicalOptions.description: Two flexible silicone rods placed in the corpora; the penis is manually positioned upward for intercourse and downward at other times. Simpler, lower-cost alternative to the inflatable device, often used when manual dexterity precludes IPP use.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Patient satisfaction 70-85% in selected series; mechanical durability over 95% at 10 years.

surgicalOptions.name: Penile revascularisation surgery
surgicalOptions.description: Microsurgical bypass of an occluded pudendal artery using inferior epigastric artery anastomosis to a deep dorsal penile vein. Narrow indication: younger men (typically under 50) with documented focal arterial injury from blunt pelvic or perineal trauma and no systemic vascular disease.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Long-term success in 60-70% of carefully selected patients; not appropriate for atherosclerotic vasculogenic ED.

surgicalOptions.name: Penile venous surgery (venous ligation)
surgicalOptions.description: Largely abandoned procedure that aimed to correct veno-occlusive dysfunction by ligating drainage veins. Long-term failure rates exceed 50% and most contemporary guidelines (AUA 2018, EAU 2021) no longer recommend it.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Initial response 40-50%, falling below 30% at 5 years; rarely offered today.

alternativeTreatments.name: Low-intensity extracorporeal shock wave therapy (Li-ESWT)
alternativeTreatments.description: Investigational therapy that delivers low-energy acoustic pulses to the penis to stimulate neovascularisation. Small randomised trials show IIEF-5 improvement of 4-7 points in mild-to-moderate vasculogenic ED; AUA 2018 (amended 2023) classifies it as investigational pending larger trials.

alternativeTreatments.name: Pelvic floor muscle training (Kegel exercises)
alternativeTreatments.description: Twelve weeks of structured pelvic floor physiotherapy improves IIEF scores in mild ED, particularly post-prostatectomy patients. Evidence base from Dorey BJUI 2004 and subsequent trials; useful adjunct rather than monotherapy.

alternativeTreatments.name: Mediterranean-style diet and structured exercise
alternativeTreatments.description: 150 minutes of moderate aerobic exercise per week plus a Mediterranean diet improves IIEF-5 scores by 2-3 points on average and reduces ED incidence by approximately 40% in cohort data (Esposito JAMA 2004).

whySeeSpecialist: Refer to a urologist or sexual medicine specialist when PDE5 inhibitors fail or are not tolerated at maximum dose, when intracavernosal injection or a penile prosthesis is being considered, when ED follows pelvic surgery or radiotherapy and rehabilitation is needed, when Peyronie's disease coexists, when there is suspected hypogonadism requiring confirmation and replacement, or when ED occurs in a young man after pelvic trauma where revascularisation may be appropriate. Primary care manages most uncomplicated ED effectively after structured cardiovascular and metabolic workup.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for board certification in urology, fellowship training or recognised experience in sexual medicine, comfort with penile duplex Doppler ultrasound, and a high-volume penile prosthesis practice (more than 25 implants per year) if surgery is being considered. Ask whether the practice offers structured post-prostatectomy penile rehabilitation, whether it routinely screens for cardiovascular risk at the ED consultation, and how it manages prosthesis infection prevention. Continuity matters — ED management often spans years and requires iterative adjustment.
preventionStrategies: Stop smoking entirely — ED incidence falls measurably within 12 months and continues to improve with longer abstinence
Achieve and maintain a body mass index below 25 — weight loss of 5-10% measurably improves IIEF-5 scores in trial data
Exercise aerobically at moderate intensity for at least 150 minutes per week — Esposito JAMA 2004 trial showed 40% lower ED incidence with structured exercise plus Mediterranean diet
Control blood pressure to under 130/80 mmHg using ED-friendly agents (ARBs, calcium-channel blockers, nebivolol) and avoiding thiazides or non-selective beta-blockers where possible
Achieve HbA1c below 7% if diabetic — every 1% reduction lowers ED progression risk and improves PDE5 inhibitor response
Treat obstructive sleep apnea with CPAP when confirmed by polysomnography — restoration of oxygenation improves nocturnal erections and IIEF-5 scores
Limit alcohol to under 14 units per week and avoid recreational drugs that impair sexual function (cocaine, MDMA, opioids, anabolic steroids)
lifestyleModifications: Adopt a Mediterranean dietary pattern emphasising vegetables, whole grains, oily fish, nuts, and olive oil — independently linked to lower ED incidence
Engage in 150-300 minutes of moderate aerobic exercise weekly, plus two resistance sessions; cycling can be added but consider seat design if symptoms relate to perineal pressure
Maintain consistent sleep of 7-9 hours per night and seek evaluation for snoring or witnessed apneas — obstructive sleep apnea is a reversible contributor
Limit alcohol intake to under 14 units per week and avoid binge episodes that suppress testosterone and erectile function
Address mental health proactively — depression, anxiety, and chronic stress independently worsen ED and reduce treatment response
Discuss medication side effects with the prescribing clinician before stopping any drug; ED-friendly substitutes exist for most cardiovascular and psychiatric drug classes
Communicate openly with the partner — partner involvement in counselling and treatment decisions is associated with substantially higher long-term satisfaction

recommended: Mediterranean diet pattern (vegetables, fruit, whole grains, olive oil, oily fish, nuts) — associated with lower ED incidence in cohort and trial data
Oily fish providing omega-3 fatty acids 2-3 times per week — improves endothelial function
Flavonoid-rich foods (berries, citrus, dark chocolate, red wine in moderation) — observational reduction in ED incidence (Cassidy AJCN 2016)
Leafy green vegetables and beets for dietary nitrates — substrate for endogenous nitric oxide production
Whole grains, legumes, and nuts as plant protein sources, replacing red and processed meats
avoid: Heavy red meat and processed meat consumption — increases cardiovascular risk and is associated with higher ED incidence in cohort data
Trans fats and ultra-processed foods — drive endothelial dysfunction
Sugar-sweetened beverages and high-fructose foods — contribute to insulin resistance and metabolic syndrome
Excess alcohol (over 14 units per week) — suppresses testosterone and worsens erectile response
Anabolic steroids, recreational nitrites ('poppers'), and other illicit substances — directly or indirectly impair sexual function and contraindicate PDE5 inhibitors

exerciseGuidelines: Regular aerobic exercise is one of the most reliable lifestyle interventions for ED. Aim for 150-300 minutes of moderate-intensity activity each week (brisk walking, swimming, cycling, jogging) plus two resistance sessions. Pelvic floor (Kegel) exercises can be added — twelve weeks of structured training improves IIEF scores in mild ED and aids post-prostatectomy recovery. Cyclists should consider saddle design and posture if symptoms correlate with perineal pressure. Exercise capacity also matters for safety — men who cannot achieve at least 3-5 metabolic equivalents (climbing two flights of stairs without symptoms) should have cardiac assessment before resuming sexual activity.
dailyManagement: Take PDE5 inhibitors as prescribed — sildenafil, vardenafil, and avanafil 30-60 minutes before sexual activity; tadalafil daily 2.5-5 mg or 10-20 mg on demand
Avoid fatty meals before sildenafil and vardenafil — fat delays absorption and blunts effect; tadalafil and avanafil are less affected
Never combine PDE5 inhibitors with nitrates (nitroglycerin, isosorbide) or recreational nitrites ('poppers') — risk of catastrophic hypotension
Use alpha-blockers (tamsulosin, alfuzosin) with care — start the PDE5 inhibitor at a low dose, separated by at least 6 hours from the alpha-blocker
Keep a record of doses tried, timing, response, and side effects — most non-responders simply require dose escalation or a switch within the class
Maintain regular cardiovascular follow-up — ED is an early signal of generalised vascular disease and warrants ongoing risk-factor management
Communicate with your partner about treatment expectations — realistic discussion improves satisfaction even when ED is only partially corrected
prognosis: Erectile dysfunction is highly treatable in the majority of men. With first-line PDE5 inhibitor therapy at optimal dose and timing, 60-80% return to satisfactory intercourse, and a further 15-20% respond to second-line injection or vacuum therapy. Approximately 90-95% of men who proceed to inflatable penile prosthesis report satisfaction with the result in long-term series (Carson 2011 and subsequent cohorts). Prognosis varies by mechanism: psychogenic and post-prostatectomy ED have the greatest potential for recovery, while ED from advanced diabetes, severe atherosclerosis, or complete cavernous nerve transection is least reversible. Importantly, ED is now recognised as an independent predictor of major adverse cardiovascular events — the 2013 Vlachopoulos meta-analysis (36,744 men, 7.5 years follow-up) showed ED carried a 44% increase in total cardiovascular events, a 25% increase in all-cause mortality, and a 19% increase in coronary heart disease. Effective ED treatment, combined with aggressive management of cardiovascular risk factors, sleep apnea, diabetes, and depression, improves both sexual outcomes and overall longevity. Most men can expect to remain sexually active into their 70s and 80s with appropriate treatment.
recoveryTimeline: PDE5 inhibitor benefit is evident on the first appropriately timed dose; an adequate trial requires at least 4-6 attempts at the maximum tolerated dose before declaring non-response. Intracavernosal injection therapy requires an in-clinic test dose and 1-2 follow-up titration visits over 2-4 weeks. Inflatable penile prosthesis surgery is a day-case or single-overnight admission; the device is activated 4-6 weeks after implantation, with full sexual activity from 6-8 weeks. Post-prostatectomy penile rehabilitation aims for recovery of spontaneous erections over 12-24 months; daily or on-demand PDE5 inhibitors plus vacuum therapy are started early after surgery. Lifestyle interventions — weight loss, exercise, smoking cessation — show measurable improvement in IIEF-5 scores by 3-6 months and continued gains at 12 months.
complications: Prolonged erection or priapism (over 4 hours) after intracavernosal injection or, rarely, after a PDE5 inhibitor — requires emergency corporal aspiration to prevent permanent cavernosal fibrosis
Severe hypotension or syncope from PDE5 inhibitor-nitrate co-administration — absolute contraindication; even single-dose nitrate use within the prior 24 hours is unsafe
Non-arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (NAION) after PDE5 inhibitors — rare (estimated 2.4 per 100,000 prescriptions) but potentially leading to permanent vision loss
Sudden sensorineural hearing loss after PDE5 inhibitors — rare but reported; stop the drug and seek otolaryngology evaluation if it occurs
Penile prosthesis infection — historically 1-3% of primary implants, now under 2% with antibiotic-coated devices (Carson 2011); requires explant and washout if it occurs
Penile prosthesis mechanical failure — under 20% at 10 years for current-generation devices; corrected by revision surgery
Untreated ED as a missed early warning of cardiovascular disease — the 3-5 year lead time before a coronary event is a key opportunity for preventive intervention
Relationship distress, depression, and avoidance of sexual intimacy when ED is unaddressed — independently worsen quality of life and treatment adherence

supportResources.description: Official patient information from the American Urological Association.

supportResources.name: AUA Guideline — Erectile Dysfunction (full text)
supportResources.description: The clinical guideline US urologists follow; freely available for patients who want to read the same evidence their clinician uses.

supportResources.name: EAU Patient Information — Male Sexual Dysfunction
supportResources.description: European Association of Urology patient-facing summary of the 2021 EAU sexual health guideline.

confusedWithConditions.name: Premature Ejaculation
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Premature ejaculation is the inability to delay ejaculation during intercourse, typically within 1-2 minutes of penetration, with intact erectile rigidity. ED, by contrast, is failure to attain or maintain rigidity. The two coexist in 30-40% of men and require separate but parallel treatment — dapoxetine or topical anaesthetics for PE, PDE5 inhibitors for ED.

confusedWithConditions.name: Peyronie's Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Peyronie's disease produces palpable tunica albuginea plaque, painful or curved erections, and may cause secondary veno-occlusive ED. ED alone does not cause curvature or palpable plaques. The two often coexist; treating curvature with collagenase clostridium histolyticum or surgery may improve erectile function.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypogonadism (Low Testosterone)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hypogonadism presents with low libido, fatigue, loss of body hair, reduced muscle mass, and depression, alongside or independent of ED. Total testosterone below 300 ng/dL on two morning samples confirms it. ED with normal testosterone and preserved libido does not require testosterone replacement.

confusedWithConditions.name: Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia (Enlarged Prostate)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: BPH causes lower urinary tract symptoms — weak stream, frequency, nocturia — without primarily affecting erections, although it commonly coexists with ED in older men. Tadalafil 5 mg daily is uniquely licensed for both indications, while alpha-blockers used for BPH can cause ejaculatory dysfunction.

confusedWithConditions.name: Depression
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Depression causes low libido, reduced interest in sex, and anhedonia, often producing situational ED. The pattern is one of reduced desire rather than mechanical failure; SSRIs add a pharmacologic ED layer. Treating the depression — often with bupropion or vortioxetine rather than SSRIs — frequently resolves the sexual symptom.

relatedConditions.name: Coronary Artery Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares atherosclerotic mechanism; ED frequently precedes overt coronary events by 3-5 years and carries a 44% increased cardiovascular event rate (Vlachopoulos 2013).

relatedConditions.name: Type 2 Diabetes
relatedConditions.relevance: Triples ED risk; affects 50-75% of men with established diabetes. Glycemic control and statin use modify the trajectory.

relatedConditions.name: High Blood Pressure
relatedConditions.relevance: Roughly doubles ED risk; thiazides and non-selective beta-blockers add iatrogenic burden, while ARBs and nebivolol are ED-neutral or favourable.

relatedConditions.name: Hypogonadism (Low Testosterone)
relatedConditions.relevance: Low testosterone reduces libido, nocturnal erections, and PDE5 inhibitor response; combined therapy improves both libido and erectile function in confirmed deficiency.

relatedConditions.name: Peyronie's Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Coexists in 20-50% of patients; tunica scarring causes mechanical and veno-occlusive ED that can require parallel surgical or intralesional treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia
relatedConditions.relevance: Coexists in older men; tadalafil 5 mg daily treats both LUTS and ED simultaneously.

faqs.question: Is erectile dysfunction curable?
faqs.answer: Many cases of ED are reversible — particularly those driven by lifestyle factors (smoking, obesity, sedentary lifestyle), medications that can be switched, hypogonadism that can be treated, and psychogenic causes. Vascular ED from established atherosclerosis is rarely reversed but is highly treatable with PDE5 inhibitors, injections, or a penile prosthesis. Around 90-95% of men reach satisfactory function with one of these options.

faqs.question: How is erectile dysfunction diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines a focused sexual and medical history, the validated IIEF-5 questionnaire, a physical examination, fasting glucose or HbA1c, a lipid panel, and a morning total testosterone when symptoms suggest hypogonadism. Most men do not need penile ultrasound; it is reserved for PDE5 inhibitor failure or pre-surgical assessment.

faqs.question: What is the most common cause of erectile dysfunction?
faqs.answer: In men over 50, the most common cause is vascular — atherosclerosis of the small penile arteries that reduces blood inflow. Diabetes, high blood pressure, high cholesterol, and smoking all accelerate this process. In men under 40, psychogenic causes and medication side effects are more common.

faqs.question: How effective are PDE5 inhibitors like sildenafil and tadalafil?
faqs.answer: PDE5 inhibitors restore satisfactory intercourse in 60-80% of men across diagnostic groups. Sildenafil produced successful intercourse in 69% of attempts versus 22% with placebo in the pivotal 1998 NEJM trial. Effectiveness is lower in advanced diabetes and post-radical prostatectomy ED but still meaningful.

faqs.question: What is the difference between sildenafil and tadalafil?
faqs.answer: Sildenafil has a 4-hour half-life and is taken 30-60 minutes before sexual activity; fatty meals delay its absorption. Tadalafil has a 17.5-hour half-life, can be taken daily at 2.5-5 mg for continuous readiness, and is the only PDE5 inhibitor licensed for coexisting benign prostatic hyperplasia symptoms.

faqs.question: Can erectile dysfunction be a sign of heart disease?
faqs.answer: Yes — ED is often the first clinical sign of generalised vascular disease and frequently precedes coronary events by 3-5 years. A 2013 meta-analysis of 36,744 men (Vlachopoulos) showed ED carried a 44% increased cardiovascular event rate and 25% higher all-cause mortality over 7 years.

faqs.question: Why can't I take Viagra with nitrates?
faqs.answer: PDE5 inhibitors potentiate the vasodilatory effect of nitrates and can cause life-threatening hypotension. This includes prescribed nitrates such as nitroglycerin and isosorbide, and recreational nitrites ('poppers'). The combination is an absolute contraindication, and even a single nitrate dose within the prior 24 hours is unsafe with sildenafil.

faqs.question: What are the side effects of PDE5 inhibitors?
faqs.answer: Common side effects include headache (10-15%), facial flushing (5-10%), nasal congestion (4-7%), dyspepsia (4-7%), and back or muscle pain (especially with tadalafil). Rare serious effects include sudden vision loss (NAION), sudden hearing loss, and priapism. Side effects usually settle with dose adjustment or switching within the class.

faqs.question: What if PDE5 inhibitors don't work for me?
faqs.answer: Around 30% of men do not respond initially to oral therapy, but many simply need dose escalation, better timing, or a switch within the class before being declared non-responders. Persistent non-responders proceed to intracavernosal injection therapy, vacuum erection devices, or, for definitive treatment, an inflatable penile prosthesis.

faqs.question: What is intracavernosal injection therapy?
faqs.answer: Intracavernosal injection delivers a vasoactive drug — most commonly alprostadil 5-40 µg or a Trimix compound — directly into the corpus cavernosum with a fine needle 5-20 minutes before intercourse. It produced successful intercourse in 87% of attempts in the pivotal Linet 1996 NEJM trial.

faqs.question: What is a penile implant and how well does it work?
faqs.answer: An inflatable penile prosthesis is a surgical implant placed inside the corpora cavernosa, with a scrotal pump and an abdominal reservoir. The patient inflates and deflates the device manually. Patient satisfaction is 90-95% and partner satisfaction 80-90% in long-term cohorts; infection rates with modern antibiotic-coated devices are under 2% (Carson 2011).

faqs.question: Does erectile dysfunction get worse with age?
faqs.answer: ED prevalence rises with age — from around 5% with complete ED at age 40 to roughly 15% at 70, and 70% reporting any-severity ED by 70 (Feldman 1994). The rise is driven by accumulating cardiovascular, metabolic, neurological, and medication burden. Treatment remains effective at every age, including in men in their 80s.

faqs.question: Can lifestyle changes alone treat erectile dysfunction?
faqs.answer: In mild-to-moderate ED, structured weight loss, Mediterranean-style diet, 150-300 minutes weekly of aerobic exercise, smoking cessation, and reduced alcohol use can improve IIEF-5 scores by 2-3 points and reduce ED incidence by approximately 40% in trial data (Esposito JAMA 2004).

faqs.question: Does low testosterone cause erectile dysfunction?
faqs.answer: Low testosterone (total <300 ng/dL on two morning samples) contributes to ED in some men, typically alongside low libido, fatigue, and reduced muscle mass. Testosterone replacement improves erectile response when combined with a PDE5 inhibitor in confirmed deficiency. In eugonadal men with ED, testosterone alone offers no demonstrated benefit.

faqs.question: Are over-the-counter or herbal ED treatments effective?
faqs.answer: Most herbal supplements marketed for ED — yohimbine, ginseng, horny goat weed — have small or inconsistent evidence and are not recommended by AUA 2018 or EAU 2021 guidelines. Some products sold online contain undeclared PDE5 inhibitors that have caused serious harm in nitrate users.

faqs.question: Can prostate surgery cause erectile dysfunction?
faqs.answer: Yes — radical prostatectomy injures or stretches the cavernous nerves that run on the posterolateral prostate. ED affects 50-80% of men in the first year even with nerve-sparing technique; recovery continues for up to 24 months. Penile rehabilitation with PDE5 inhibitors and vacuum therapy is started early to preserve cavernosal smooth muscle until neural recovery.

faqs.question: Will erectile dysfunction affect my fertility?
faqs.answer: ED itself does not impair sperm production or quality, but it can prevent intercourse and conception. Once erections are restored with PDE5 inhibitors or other therapy, fertility outcomes match age-matched peers. Concurrent testosterone replacement, however, suppresses sperm production and should be managed alongside a fertility specialist if conception is desired.

faqs.question: Can young men get erectile dysfunction?
faqs.answer: Yes — about 8% of men aged 20-39 report ED in US data (Selvin 2007). Causes in young men are more often psychogenic (performance anxiety, depression), drug-related, related to anabolic steroid or opioid use, or post-pelvic-trauma vascular injury. Workup mirrors older men but with particular attention to mental health and substance use.

faqs.question: Is erectile dysfunction more common in diabetes?
faqs.answer: Yes — type 2 diabetes triples ED risk and affects 50-75% of men with established disease, often a decade earlier than non-diabetic peers. Mechanisms include autonomic neuropathy, endothelial dysfunction, and reduced nitric oxide bioavailability. Tight glycemic control (HbA1c under 7%) and statin therapy modify the trajectory.

faqs.question: What should I do if my erection lasts more than 4 hours?
faqs.answer: An erection lasting more than 4 hours is priapism — a urological emergency. Permanent cavernosal damage and fibrosis can develop after 6 hours. Go to the nearest emergency department immediately. Initial treatment is corporal aspiration of blood with or without dilute phenylephrine injection. Priapism is more common after injection therapy than after PDE5 inhibitors.

faqs.question: How much does erectile dysfunction treatment cost?
faqs.answer: Generic sildenafil and tadalafil cost roughly USD 1-3 per tablet in the US and far less in many other countries. Intracavernosal injections cost USD 20-50 per dose. Inflatable penile prosthesis surgery is typically USD 15,000-25,000 in the US, usually covered by Medicare and most private insurers when medically indicated. In India, generic PDE5 inhibitors are widely affordable.

faqs.question: Can I drink alcohol while taking Viagra?
faqs.answer: Moderate alcohol (1-2 drinks) does not significantly interact with PDE5 inhibitors, but heavier intake worsens erections directly and increases hypotension and headache risk. Binge drinking also impairs cavernosal smooth muscle response and reduces treatment success. Limiting intake to under 14 units per week is consistent with both general health and ED treatment guidance.

metaDescription: Erectile dysfunction: vascular, neurogenic, or hormonal causes of erection failure. AUA-aligned diagnosis, PDE5 inhibitors, injections, implants reviewed.
